content:
# Matthew Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/MAT_011_page_001.jpg, MAT_011_page_002.jpg

1. And it came to pass when the Yeshua finished commanding the twelve disciples of him, he departed from there to teach and to proclaim in the cities of them.

2. And the Ioannes, having heard in the prison the works of the Christos, having sent through the disciples of him,

3. said to him, "Are you the coming [one], or do we look for another?"

4. And answering, the Yeshua said to them, "Having gone, report to Ioannes what you hear and see:

5. [the] blind see again and [the] lame walk, lepers are cleansed and [the] deaf hear, and [the] dead are raised and [the] poor are given-good-news.

6. And blessed is whoever is not scandalized in me."

7. And of these going, the Yeshua began to say to the crowds concerning Ioannes, "What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed being shaken by [the] wind?

8. But what did you go out to see? A man in soft [garments] having been clothed? Behold, the [ones] wearing the soft [things] are in the houses of the kings.

9. But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and more than a prophet.

10. This is [the one] concerning whom it has been written: 'Behold, I send the messenger of me before the face of you, who will prepare the way of you before you.'

11. Truly I say to you, there has not risen among [those] born of women [one] greater than Ioannes the Baptizer; but the least in the kingdom of the heavens is greater than he.

12. And from the days of Ioannes the Baptizer until now, the kingdom of the heavens is being violently-seized, and violent [ones] seize it.

13. For all the prophets and the law prophesied until Ioannes.

14. And if you are willing to receive [it], he is Elias, the [one] being about to come.

15. The [one] having ears, let him hear.

16. But to what shall I liken this generation? It is like children sitting in the marketplaces, calling to the others,

17. saying, 'We played the flute for you and you did not dance; we sang-a-dirge and you did not mourn.'

18. For Ioannes came neither eating nor drinking, and they say, 'He has a demon.'

19. The Son of the Man came eating and drinking, and they say, 'Behold, a man [who is] a glutton and a wine-drinker, a friend of tax-collectors and sinners.' And wisdom was justified from the works of her."

20. Then he began to reproach the cities in which most of the mighty-works of him had happened, because they did not repent.

21. "Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if in Tyre and Sidon had happened the mighty-works having happened in you, long ago in sackcloth and ashes they would have repented.

22. But I say to you, for Tyre and Sidon it will be more tolerable in [the] day of judgment than for you.

23. And you, Capernaum, will you be exalted to heaven? To Hades you will descend. For if in Sodom had happened the mighty-works having happened in you, it would have remained until today.

24. But I say to you that for [the] land of Sodom it will be more tolerable in [the] day of judgment than for you."

25. In that season, answering, the Yeshua said, "I praise you, Father, Lord of the heaven and of the earth, that you hid these things from [the] wise and understanding and revealed them to infants.

26. Yes, Father, for thus it was well-pleasing before you.

27. All things were delivered to me by the Father of me, and no one knows the Son except the Father, nor does anyone know the Father except the Son and [the one] to whom the Son wills to reveal [him].

28. Come to me, all the [ones] laboring and having been burdened, and I will give rest to you.

29. Take the yoke of me upon you and learn from me, for I am gentle and humble in the heart, and you will find rest for the souls of you.

30. For the yoke of me [is] easy, and the burden of me is light."

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **βιάζεται (v.12):** "Is being violently-seized" — this verb can be middle ("forces its way") or passive ("suffers violence"). The Sinaiticus form is ambiguous. EMET preserves the passive reading. The βιασταί ("violent ones") who "seize" it could be positive (the eager) or negative (opponents).

2. **ἔργων (v.19):** Sinaiticus reads "works" (ἔργων) — "wisdom was justified from her works." Some manuscripts read "children" (τέκνων). The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes that wisdom's deeds are her vindication.

3. **Ἠλίας (v.14):** "Elias" — the Greek form of Elijah. Jesus identifies John the Baptist with the expected return of Elijah (Malachi 4:5), but qualifies it with "if you are willing to receive it."

4. **vv.28-30 — The Great Invitation:** The "yoke" (ζυγός) is a rabbinic metaphor for Torah teaching. Every rabbi had a "yoke" — his interpretation of the law. Jesus offers his yoke as an alternative: gentle, humble, restful.

— EMET, AI Translator
